title: Dog Cut on Ear Flap Bleeding: First Aid and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your dog's ear is bleeding. Learn about first aid, urgency levels, and why a photo is essential for veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ear flap injuries often cause significant bleeding but are rarely life-threatening; apply direct pressure and seek veterinary care within 24 hours to prevent complications.

What is an ear flap injury in dogs?

An ear flap injury, or a cut to the pinna, involves damage to the thin skin and cartilage of your dog's ear. Because the ears are filled with many tiny blood vessels, even a minor scratch can lead to significant bleeding. This often looks scary, as the blood may splatter when your dog shakes their head, but the primary goal is to remain calm and manage the bleeding.

Is a bleeding ear flap injury an emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While a bleeding ear is rarely life-threatening, it is difficult to heal without professional help.
  • If the bleeding is heavy and does not stop after 10 minutes of direct pressure, it becomes a high-priority emergency.
  • Otherwise, schedule a veterinary visit within 12 to 24 hours to prevent infection or the formation of an aural hematoma from head shaking.

How does taking a photo of the wound help with triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows a triage professional to assess the depth, length, and exact location of the cut remotely.
  • Visuals help the veterinary team determine if the wound requires surgical glue, stitches, or a professional bandage.
  • Taking a photo ensures your pet receives the correct level of care immediately.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Hemorrhage should be controlled with mild pressure, and additional material can be placed over the original bandage if it becomes soaked with blood. If bleeding from the ear flap is venous (dark, oozing), elevation above the heart is recommended. Suturing large ear lacerations is usually not effective and not recommended; if damage is severe, ear tissue may require significant debridement or partial surgical removal.

Chapter: Surgery, Emergency, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2009)

faqs.question: How do I stop my dog's ear from bleeding right now?
faqs.answer: Apply firm, continuous pressure to the site of the bleeding using a clean towel or gauze for at least 5 to 10 minutes. Avoid lifting the gauze to check the wound, as this can break the newly formed clot.

faqs.question: Can I use flour or cornstarch to stop the bleeding?
faqs.answer: In an emergency, clean cornstarch or flour can help clot minor nicks, but it is better to use pressure. Avoid using these if the cut is deep, as they can cause irritation or infection inside the wound.

faqs.question: Why does my dog keep shaking their head?
faqs.answer: Dogs shake their heads because the sensation of blood or a wound on the ear is irritating. This shaking often reopens the wound. You can gently wrap the ear against the dog's head with a soft bandage to prevent further damage.
